era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Amenhotep III. The Hittite Empire under Mursili II is consolidating power. Mycenaean Greece is in LHIIIB. The Levant is under Egyptian suzerainty. This period represents the apex of Late Bronze Age international relations and trade.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign at its height
events.description: Amenhotep III's reign continues to represent the apex of Egyptian power and prosperity. His long reign (c. 1390–1352 BCE) saw Egypt at the height of its territorial extent and economic power. His diplomatic correspondence with foreign rulers (preserved in the Amarna Letters, though dated to his successor's reign) demonstrates Egypt's central role in Late Bronze Age international relations.
events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-established through multiple sources.

events.title: Shang Dynasty oracle bone divination and administration
events.description: The Shang Dynasty in China continues its dominance of the Yellow River valley. Oracle bone inscriptions from this period record divination practices, royal hunts, and administrative matters. The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) represents the first historically documented Chinese civilization, though the exact dating of individual years remains uncertain.
events.certainty_note: The Shang Dynasty's existence is confirmed, but the exact dating of individual years is uncertain. Oracle bones provide contemporary records, but their chronological placement is debated.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Amenhotep III's reign remains subject to scholarly debate, with some Egyptologists placing it slightly earlier or later depending on astronomical calculations and radiocarbon dating. The 'high' and 'low' chronologies for the Late Bronze Age differ by up to 50–100 years. Additionally, the extent of Egyptian control over the Levant is sometimes overstated in Egyptian sources; some scholars argue that Egyptian hegemony was more nominal than direct.
historiographic_note: For 1311 BCE, written records are limited to Egypt, the Hittite Empire, and fragmentary evidence from China. Egyptian sources dominate and are often propagandistic. The Shang Dynasty oracle bones provide contemporary records but are difficult to date precisely. The vast majority of human societies leave no synchronizable written record.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III's reign continues into the next year.
